# 2026-07-07 Core30 Updates, GBP & Ask Maps

- Code: 4ffc1bc4
- Source: https://www.skool.com/ai-seo-mastery-pro/classroom/52e05dc7?md=4ffc1bc45d0b4fd7890cfcaf90cca038
- Video: https://vimeo.com/1207882248?share=copy&fl=sv&fe=ci
- Duration: 93m 39s

## Description

# Weekly Q&A Call Summary — July 7, 2026

## 📌 Executive Summary

This week's Q&A focused heavily on major upcoming changes to the Core30 agent: canonical JSON output, a built-in website builder, publishing to nearly any platform, and a move away from bring-your-own API keys toward a larger free tier. Caleb also covered GBP best practices (re-verification risk, categories, spam calls), realtor SEO strategy, review request tactics, and how Ask Maps and AI agents are likely to reshape local search over the next 1-2 years.

## 🔑 Key Takeaways

**Big Core30 updates are coming fast.** Canonical JSON output rolls out this week, with a website builder and platform-agnostic publishing (Wix, Webflow, GHL, WordPress Gutenberg blocks, and more) targeted by the end of July.

**API keys are going away.** The Core30 agent will drop BYOK entirely. The free tier jumps from ~12 to 50-60 articles per month, so ~95% of users will pay less or nothing. Power users will pay a modest ~50% token markup ($0.75-$1.50 per article).

**Owner-sent review requests convert ~10x better than automated ones** (~46% vs. under 4%). Have owners text customers personally and ask them to "talk about what happened and the outcome."

**Press release + citations 30 days before video verification** raises the GBP verification success rate from roughly 10% to 90%+.

**Content and links together are the formula** — each alone is only about 10% as effective. Every page published or significantly edited should get a link sourced to it.

## 📊 Main Discussion Points

### Core30 Agent: Canonical JSON, Website Builder & Publishing

Every block of content (intros, headings, images, FAQs) will be tagged with canonical JSON — code that tells Google and website platforms exactly what each piece is.

This unlocks two things: the Core30 agent can build websites itself (including via Claude Code), and it can publish directly to almost any platform. Adding a new platform takes ~4 hours once JSON is live; request a platform and it can be added within days to weeks.

WordPress publishing will switch from raw HTML dumps to native Gutenberg blocks, so pages match your theme and are easy to edit. Gutenberg turned out to be ~10x harder than other platforms, so solving it made everything else easy.

The long-term vision: one piece of software that handles research, writing, publishing, and website management for local SEO. Phase 4 (link building) and phase 5 (a simple built-in CRM) are being scoped. A future MCP connection to Claude and other AI platforms is on the table.

Coming soon: connect Core30 to Google Search Console to find attributes you're indexed for but don't mention on your site (e.g., "coffee shop with fast Wi-Fi"), then add them automatically — built for AI attribute matching.

Also in the works: a revamped client-settings filler that can ingest any documentation, crawl your website, and even interview you by voice for missing details. Tip: you can already paste an onboarding call transcript into client settings so content reflects the client's unique knowledge.

Smaller items: a light theme is being added, low-value AI detection options (Winston, GPTZero) are being removed in favor of ZeroGPT, and agencies with teams currently share one login since access is tied to Skool membership.

### Pricing Change: Goodbye BYOK

~90% of recent errors and ~80% of setup questions come from user API keys (wrong keys, empty credits, rate limits on new keys). The agent currently writes ~2,000 articles a week across the community.

The new model: free up to 50-60 articles per month (up from ~12). Above that, usage is billed with a ~50% token markup — about $0.75-$1.50 per article instead of $0.50-$1.00 with your own key.

Anyone under ~85 articles a month will actually pay less than they do today. Caleb asked for feedback from anyone unhappy with the change.

### GBP Management: Re-verification, Categories & Spam

The three changes most likely to trigger re-verification: business name, primary category, and address (better than 50/50 odds each). Everything else, including phone number, is lower risk — but never zero. Always warn clients before touching categories, services, phone, or website so they're ready to film a re-verification.

Category myth busted: adding secondary categories and services does NOT dilute your primary category. Use as many relevant categories and services as possible — just don't keyword stuff. Pick GMB Everywhere suggestions based on relevance to the client's actual services, not usage rates.

Google Voice numbers on a GBP attract heavy spam. Keep the GBP number matching the website number; consider a CRM with spam filtering (LeadSnap's CRM was built for this).

Multi-location businesses: keep categories and services identical only if every location does the same things; otherwise match each location's actual offerings.

### Moving or New GBP Locations

30 days before video verification: publish citations and a press release with the new address, plus a GBP post announcing the move. The goal is that Google's manual reviewer finds plenty of search results tying the address to the business name.

Data point: verification success went from ~10% without this prep to 90%+ with it. A member confirmed 2-for-2 success on private-residence verifications after press releases.

### Realtor SEO Strategy

Million-dollar sellers rarely Google for realtors, but many people now ask ChatGPT for recommendations — so frame the offer around getting recommended by AI systems (the actual work is nearly identical).

Target hyper-specific neighborhood content (~95% location pages, ~5% service pages): pricing, who the area fits and doesn't, alternative neighborhoods, and honest challenges. That's what people ask AI about.

The content must be informationally additive — things not already on the SERP. Interview the realtor and feed the transcript into Core30's client settings so their unique local knowledge shows up in the content.

GBP management is still a fine low-ticket offer for realtors; just set expectations.

### Ranking in Competitive Markets & Suburbs

If your GBP address is in a suburb, ranking in the neighboring major city is nearly impossible — proximity and the searcher's city matter enormously. Caleb's agency tells suburb clients to get an office in the main city first.

Small bordering towns are the opposite: fewer competitors means rank can carry across several small towns much more easily than suburb-into-big-city.

Set goals using the top competitor's rank map size. In dense markets the winning rank map may only be 5x5 miles.

Pages "crawled, not indexed" = Google judging the content as low value. Fix the content quality first (Google's April update targets non-additive AI content), then source a link when you republish.

Link strategy: order a link every time you publish or significantly edit a page. Cheap Web 2.0/PBN links (QGP, Legiit) are fine; spend the bigger budget on real local links — youth sports sponsorships, chambers of commerce, charities.

### Reviews, Proposals & Agency Operations

Review requests: owner texts personally from a familiar number (~46% conversion) vs. automation (under 4%). Ask customers to "talk about what happened and the outcome" so attributes appear naturally. Rotate platforms (Yelp, Angi) since Gemini values non-GBP reviews.

Proposals: same structure for every client; just swap goals, scope, and light competitor notes. The $2,400/month minimum covers 12 articles monthly (8 in month one due to GBP setup).

Team workflow case study: Core30 collapsed a multi-person pipeline (researcher → briefs → copywriters → VA for images/schema → developer → review) into one content manager handling 300-400 articles a month. Per-article review time dropped from ~22 minutes to ~6, including schema. Every article is still human-reviewed before publishing.

Hiring a content manager for this workflow? Prioritize editing experience above all else.

Lead close rates: ~35-40% from ads (phone sales), ~50%+ now from YouTube-sourced leads at higher prices. "The best way to grow your SEO agency is to start a YouTube channel two years ago."

Client retention is currently ~96%.

### Schema & Yoast Conflicts

Yoast can only toggle schema site-wide, which conflicts with custom schema on large existing sites. Rank Math is now preferred because it allows per-page schema control.

For a big legacy Yoast site: hire a WordPress developer (e.g., via Upwork) to enable custom schema on chosen pages, or use a Screaming Frog crawl + Claude Code to generate schema in bulk.

### Rank Map Accuracy

All third-party rank trackers use geo-located IP searches, which Google actively fights — so LeadSnap, Local Falcon, and Local Dominator will all show similar-but-different results, and none matches your phone exactly.

Your own searches are skewed by history — even incognito mode is influenced by prior browsing, and results differ by device (iPhone vs. Android in the same car).

Practical advice: pick one rank map tool and stick with it so clients see consistent trends; validate with real signals like calls and impressions.

### Ask Maps, Gemini & the Future of Local Search

Ask Maps (launched March) lets users chat with Gemini about local businesses — a strong signal Google will bring Gemini into local results. The three-pack algorithm hasn't meaningfully changed in 10+ years.

Agency adjustments already in the pro group workflow: monthly press releases via PR Underground (~$80, hits Google News); [PRASAP.com](http://PRASAP.com) for featured local newspaper/TV articles (~$1,500) in competitive markets; and AI-generated videos on a third to half of all content, uploaded to YouTube and embedded — because Gemini is the only frontier model natively trained on YouTube.

If Gemini fully replaces the three-pack: PBN budgets (~$300-400/month) would shift to press releases and PRASAP. If it's a hybrid, PBNs stay. Biggest losers: businesses ranking on proximity alone. Biggest winners: multi-location brands with deep authority.

Sundar Pichai has described Google's future as an "agent manager" — Google AI agents already call local businesses to check product stock. Human website traffic may plummet within 12-24 months as content is increasingly written for AI agents.

Caleb's 5-year view: SEO will still be content and links, because every platform (Google, ChatGPT, Gemini) must build trust in businesses using only their online presence.

## 💡 Notable Q&A

**Q: How will the Core30 website builder work — do we use our own Claude API?** A: No. BYOK is being removed entirely. Canonical JSON output enables both the builder and direct publishing to nearly any platform, with a bigger free tier replacing API keys.

**Q: How risky is changing a phone number on a GBP?** A: Lower than 50/50 for re-verification, but never zero. Name, primary category, and address are the big triggers. Always prep the client before major edits.

**Q: Why avoid putting pages in the navigation bar if we're not trying to rank them?** A: Nav, footer, and no-follow links pass zero authority but still count toward the total link count — so they dilute the authority flowing to the links that matter.

**Q: I'm writing supporting content but rankings aren't moving. What's wrong?** A: Usually content quality (de-indexing = Google sees no value) or missing local links. Fix the content first, then source a link with every publish. Prioritize real local sponsorships over expensive PBN packages.

**Q: Should a home care business set its primary category to what it can sell now (cleaning) or its long-term goal (home care)?** A: It depends, but Caleb's agency now recommends whatever gets the client calls fastest — a green rank map keeps clients happy and funded, even if the long-term path earns more on paper. One established GBP beats adding a second.

**Q: Will Ask Maps kill PBNs and shift everything to brand mentions?** A: Likely a gradual hybrid, not a flip. Keep current tactics but ensure everything also builds AI visibility: press releases, local media features, and YouTube videos embedded in content.

## 🔮 Emerging Trends & Challenges

Google's April update aggressively de-indexes AI content that isn't informationally additive — average AI output no longer even gets indexed.

Gemini appears headed into the local map pack; proximity-based "free lunch" rankings may disappear, favoring businesses with real authority.

AI agents (not humans) will increasingly visit websites and even phone businesses — content may soon be written primarily for AI agents.

Gemini weighs signals beyond the GBP: YouTube videos, non-Google reviews, and news mentions all matter more.

Attribute matching is growing in importance — AI systems match specific amenities and details (e.g., fast Wi-Fi) far more than classic Google ranking did.

Rank tracking accuracy keeps degrading as Google fights geo-spoofed searches; treat all rank maps as directional.

## 🛠 Tools & Resources Mentioned

**Core30 agent** — content, publishing, upcoming website builder, GSC attribute matching

**LeadSnap** — rank maps and spam-filtering CRM

**GMB Everywhere** — category research

**Rank Math** (preferred) and **Yoast** — WordPress SEO/schema plugins

**Screaming Frog** — site crawls for bulk schema generation

**Claude Code** — bulk schema generation and site builds

**QGP / Legiit** — budget link sourcing

**PR Underground** (~$80/mo) — press releases that hit Google News

**[PRASAP.com](http://PRASAP.com)** (~$1,500) — featured articles in local newspapers/TV for competitive markets

**ZeroGPT** — AI detection (Winston and GPTZero being removed as options)

**Ask Maps** ([maps.google](http://maps.google)) — Gemini-powered local business chat

**Google Search Console** — upcoming Core30 attribute-matching integration

**Pro training** — Caleb's copywriter training, $397 project breakdown, Facebook Ads module with review-request contract language

**Write Your First Article** — Mark Farrow's Thursday Core30 onboarding session
